issues.description: Crick coined the term 'central dogma' in a 1957 talk and published the formulation in 1958, but the famous one-page sketch was actually a 1956 note. Saying he 'sketched it on a single page in 1958' is a small historical conflation. Fix: say 'Crick articulated the central dogma in 1958' and drop the 'single page' line.

The 'one gene, one messenger, one protein' framing is the discarded 'one gene–one polypeptide' shorthand and contradicts the alternative-splicing point made two sections later. A student will get whiplash. Fix: say 'one gene can be copied into one or more messengers, each translated into a protein.'

'TATA box about 25 base pairs upstream' is fine, but the script says the TATA box 'marks' the promoter — the TATA box is a core promoter element, not a marker upstream of the promoter. Phrase as 'the core promoter, which contains the TATA box ~25 bp upstream of the transcription start site.'

The spliceosome is described as 'a complex of small nuclear RNAs.' It is composed of snRNPs — small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNAs + proteins), not RNA alone. AP exam stems sometimes test snRNP terminology.

'Humans make over a hundred thousand different proteins' from ~20,000 genes is a popular but unsupported number. Current proteomics estimates the human proteome at roughly 20,000 canonical proteins with hundreds of thousands of proteoforms once PTMs are included; the often-quoted '100,000 proteins from alternative splicing alone' is not a defensible figure. Fix: 'one gene can produce multiple protein isoforms via alternative splicing, dramatically expanding the proteome,' without a fabricated number. Also, rice has ~35,000–50,000 genes, not 'fewer than humans' in the way implied — the throwaway comparison is misleading.
Prokaryotic ribosome subunit detail is missing — it's 50S + 30S = 70S. Saying only '70S total' is fine for AP but worth one extra clause for symmetry with the eukaryotic breakdown.

Section claims 'every protein begins life with a methionine at position one.' True at translation initiation, but the initiator Met is frequently cleaved post-translationally, and in prokaryotes the initiator is fMet (formyl-methionine). For AP this is acceptable, but the absolute phrasing 'every protein' is too strong. Also: anticodons and wobble are mentioned in the CED concept but not explained in narration.

Worked-example error. Template strand given: 3'-TAC GGC ATT-5'. Complementary antiparallel mRNA should be 5'-AUG CCG UAA-3'. The script gets that right. BUT then it says 'CCG codes for proline.' CCG codes for PROLINE — correct. Good. However, the script then says 'the finished peptide is just two amino acids long — methionine, then proline' — this is fine. No error here on recheck. Downgrading: the only quibble is that release factors recognize stop codons in the A site and the peptide hydrolyzes off the P-site tRNA; 'pops the chain off' is acceptable shorthand.

lac operon description omits the catabolite-repression layer (cAMP-CAP boosts transcription when glucose is low), which IS in the CED reference and which AP free-response questions love. Worth one sentence.

'DNA methylation represses and histone acetylation activates' is broadly correct for AP, but the script should clarify DNA methylation specifically at CpG islands of promoters (per CED) — otherwise students get tripped up by gene-body methylation contradictions on harder stems.

Sickle-cell description is correct (GAG→GTG, Glu→Val, β-globin position 6). Minor nit: this is a DNA-level mutation; at the mRNA level it is GAG→GUG. Worth noting to avoid confusion when students see the mRNA version on the exam.

summary: Script is largely sound for AP Biology Unit 6 and aligned to CED content. The most exam-relevant fix is the '100,000 proteins from 20,000 genes' claim in section 07 — that number is not defensible and the rice comparison is sloppy. Several minor polish items (snRNP terminology, cAMP-CAP, CpG specificity) would tighten alignment with the CED.
